coredata.dc:title: Fluorescence enhancement of bovine serum albumin gold nanoclusters from La3+ ion: Detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+)
coredata.dc:creator: Desai, Mittal L.
Basu, Hirakendu
Saha, Sudeshna
Singhal, Rakesh Kumar
Kailasa, Suresh Kumar
coredata.dc:description: The combination of La3+ ion and bovine serum albumin gold nanoclusters (La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs)-based nanosensor creates a novel and innovative miniaturized analytical method for the detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) simultaneously. In this work, the fluorescence enhancement was noticed in BSA-AuNCs by the addition of La3+ ion, exhibiting emission peak at 652 nm when excited at 323 nm. The fabricated La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs acted as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) via fluorescence quenching (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) and fluorescence enhancement (Cd2+) mechanisms. The La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescent nanosensor exhibited good linearity with limits of detection (LOD) of 0.02, 0.048, 0.19 and 4.93 µM for Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions, respectively. The practical application of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescence method was examined for the analysis of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) in various environmental water samples. Thus, the developed probe exhibited several merits such as miniaturization, easy of operation, which allow to use as a promising analytical platform for on-site parallel detection of four divalent metal ions in real samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: La3+ion-BSA-AuNCs
Florescence detection
Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorescence assay of four divalent metal ions
Analysis of four divalent metal ions in real samples
Fluorescence detection of four divalent metal ions
Fluorescence assay of four divalent metal ions using La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs
Selectivity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs and masking target metal ions
Fluorescence assays of four divalent metal ions in water samples

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors grateful thank the Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences (BRNS) project (37(2)/14/07/2015/BRNS/10401), Department of Atomic Energy, Government of India, India. MLD and SKK thank the Director, SVNIT, Surat for providing necessary facilities.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: High resolution HR-TEM images of HR-TEM images of (a) La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs (b) the diameter of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs estimated from HR-TEM image (c) distinct single La3+ -ion-BSA-AuNCs particle with lattice fringes of 0.12 nm. La3+ -ion-BSA-AuNCs with (d) Hg2+ and (e) Pb2+ ions.
Comparison of excitation and emission spectra of BSA-AuNCs with and without La3+ ion.
Absorption, excitation and emission spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs. Inset: Photograph of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs under UV lamp at 365 nm.

Fluorescence spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs (λex = 323 nm) upon the addition of various metal ions (Hg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cr3+ and As3+, 0.5 mM). Inset: Photograph of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs with above metal ions under UV light at 365 nm.

Emission spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs (λex = 323 nm) in the presence of (a) Hg2+ ion (0.01–500 µM) and (b) Cu2+ ion (0.075–500 µM). Insets show the photographs of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs with different concentrations of Hg2+ and Cu2+ ion under illumination of UV light at 365 nm.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of La3+ hybrid Au NCs with various concentration of Pb2+ ranging from 1.0 to 500 µM (b) Calibration curve for the quantification of Pb2+ ion using La3+ hybrid Au NCs (linear range of the graph 2.5–60 µm).
Masking of Cu2+ and Cd2+ ions using thioureia and KI as masking agents during the detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions.

Schematic illustrations representing the overall the development of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescence method for the simultaneous detection of Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+ ions via fluorescence quenching and of Cd2+ ion via fluorescence enhancement.
Comparison of present method for sensing of Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions with reported methods.

row.entry: Required EDTA to etched the surface of QDs
Thiocyanate, 3-mercaptopropeionic acid, thiourea and KI were used as masking agents

head.ce:title: Fluorescence enhancement of bovine serum albumin gold nanoclusters from La3+ ion: Detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Sardar Vallabhbhai National Institute of Technology, Surat 395 007, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Center, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Analytical Chemistry Division, Bhabha Atomic Research Center, Trombay, Mumbai 400085, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The combination of La3+ ion and bovine serum albumin gold nanoclusters (La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs)-based nanosensor creates a novel and innovative miniaturized analytical method for the detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) simultaneously. In this work, the fluorescence enhancement was noticed in BSA-AuNCs by the addition of La3+ ion, exhibiting emission peak at 652 nm when excited at 323 nm. The fabricated La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs acted as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) via fluorescence quenching (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) and fluorescence enhancement (Cd2+) mechanisms. The La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescent nanosensor exhibited good linearity with limits of detection (LOD) of 0.02, 0.048, 0.19 and 4.93 µM for Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions, respectively. The practical application of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescence method was examined for the analysis of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) in various environmental water samples. Thus, the developed probe exhibited several merits such as miniaturization, easy of operation, which allow to use as a promising analytical platform for on-site parallel detection of four divalent metal ions in real samples.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid industrialization and excess use of inorganic chemicals, water is contaminated by various toxic metal ions, causing serious health issues to humans [1–2]. Among divalent metal ions, three divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Pb2+ andCd2+) are considered to be highly toxic species and significantly inhibited various biochemical pathways in the living organisms of humans, which causes several disorders [3–5]. For example, Hg2+ ion causes serious neural disorders including Alzheimer’s Hunter-Rusell syndrome, and Minamata [6,7]. Similarly, Pb2+ ion is considered as a toxic element due to its adverse effects onto the central nervous system and brain of humans, causing various neurological diseases [8]. Cd2+ ion has been recognized as a class 1 toxic element, causing serious health effects on the various key organs (kidney and liver) of humans via inhibiting various molecular reactions in the cells [9]. Noticeably, Cu2+ ion is one of the important bioactive metal ion for various cellular reactions, exhibiting an essential role in various biochemical reactions [10]. However, it may toxic nature to the living system when its concentration is in abnormal, causing various disorders [11]. In view of this, the maximum permissible levels of 0.002 mg/L, 20 μM, 75 nm, and 3 μg/L for Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions, respectively recommended by the United States Environmental Protection Agency (EPA) and World Health Organisation (WHO) in drinking water. Thus, a simple analytical method is highly demanded for simultaneous monitoring of four divalent metal ions in wastewater, environmental and food samples. Until now, a wide variety of analytical techniques including atomic absorption spectrometry, Raman spectroscopy, electrochemical, inductively coupled plasma (ICP)- optical emission spectrometry (OES) and mass spectrometric (MS) techniques have been successfully used for the detection of four divalent metal ions in various samples [12–17]. Although some of the methods exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity for the analysis of four divalent metal ions, but they are relatively expensive and required specific ligands, solvents and tedious sample preparations, which limits their use in screening of toxic metal ions in various samples. Thus, the development of inexpensive and portable analytical method is highly desirable for simultaneous detection of four divalent metal ions.

In recent years, fluorescent metal NCs have emerged as unique class of nanosensors with outstanding analytical applications for assaying of wide variety of target analytes [18–20]. Among metal NCs, AuNCs have emerged as promising nanoprobes for the development of portable fluorescence analytical methods for the detection of various trace level chemical species [21–25]. With compared to gold nanoparticles, AuNCs exhibit exceptional and tailorable optical properties because of their small size (<3.0 nm) [26]. The dimension of AuNCs is very close to Fermi wavelength, thereby the energy level spacing is increased with decreasing the radius of AuNCs, which results size-dependent electronic structure [27]. Since gold center of AuNCs contains a precise number of Au atoms, favouring to behave as molecular-like species or superatoms. As a result, AuNCs exhibited distinct superior optical properties (high emission rates, good photostability, large Stokes shift, size-dependent tunable emission and strong photoluminescence) as compared to quantum dots, thereby favoring them to use as optimum fluorescent sensors for the recognition of various chemical species [28]. To fabricate fluorescent AuNCs, several biopolymers and ligands including bovine serum albumin (BSA), lysozyme, human serum albumin, trypsin, pepsin, amylase, β-lactoglobulin, transferrin, 16-Mer ferritin, glutathione-histidine and Curcuma longa extract [3,23,24,29–36] have been used as potential ligands for the generation of fluorescent AuNCs and applied as outstanding candidates for various applications. In this study, BSA was selected as a reducing as well as surface passivating agent for the fabrication of AuNCs due to its water solubility, affordability, biocompatibility and good ligand chemistry properties, allowing to use as potential ligand for the preparation of AuNCs-based fluorescent sensor [29]. Noticeably, AuNCs-based fluorescence methods have proven to be simple analytical tools for assaying of various chemical species, offering higher sensitivity as compared to other analytical techniques [25]. To enhance fluorescence emission of AuNCs, various metal ions (Zn2+, La3+, Eu3+), lanthanids and metal–organic framework have been introduced into AuNCs for the enhancement of fluorescence intensity, exhibiting as promising agents for sensing and bioimaging applications [23,37–44]. Moreover, the rare-earth elements exhibit unique atomic structure, and are widely used as important precursors for the preparation of fluorescent nanomaterials, facilitating to apply them as probes in bio- imaging and sensing studies [45]. It was noticed that the addition of Er3+ ion as dopant was remarkably enhanced upconverted emission of NCs [46]. To improve the optical properties of NCs, La3+ ion is used as a dopant in the synthesis of BSA-AuNCs, which can enhance the fluorescence properties of BSA-AuNCs.

Herein, we report the enhancement of fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs by the addition of La3+ ion, exhibiting good quantum yield (QY) and water-dispersability. The La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs exhibited red fluorescence and showed strong emission peak at 652. Importantly, La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs acted as a fluorescent sensor for assaying of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) via fluorescence quenching (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) and fluorescence enhancement (Cd2+) mechanisms (Scheme 1 ). The fluorescence emission spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-Au NCs were greatly quenched by three divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) and enhancement by Cd2+ ion. The magnitude of the emission intensity changes upon the addition of above metal ions leads to establish quantitative measurement for four divalent metal ions. Further, La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs fluorescent nanosensor was successfully applied for assaying of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) in various water samples.

BSA and metal salts (HAuCl4·4H2O,(Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Cu(NO3)2·3H2O, Hg(NO3)2·H2O, AgNO3 and As2O3) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. Lanthanum nitrate (La(NO3)3) was purchased from Finar laboratories. Milli-Q ultrapure water was used for the preparation of AuNCs and analytical experiments.

UV–visible absorption spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs were measured on a Maya Pro 2000 spectrophotometer (Ocean Optics, USA). Excitation and emission spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs were recorded by using Carry Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Agilent Technologies). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) was used to measure the morphology of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs.

The typical microwave synthetic route was established for the fabrication of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs using different ratios of precursors (Au3+/BSA/La3+ ion). Briefly, 2.5 mL of BSA (125 mg/mL) and 3 mM of La(NO3)3 solutions were added to 2.5 mL of 10 mM HAuCl4 solution, followed by the addition of 0.5 mL of 1.0 M NaOH. The above solution was stirred (450 rpm) for 5.0 min at room temperature. The solution was kept into domestic microwave oven and then applied 120 W for 100 s. The solution was turned into light yellow to brown color, emitting a strong red fluorescence under UV light, which confirms the formation of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs. In order to investigate the effect of La3+ ion on the fluorescence enhancement of BSA-AuNCs, the different concentrations of La3+ ion was introduced into BSA-AuNCs and studied their emission spectra. The as prepared La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs were stored in refrigerator at 4 °C and their analytical applications were investigated.

The QY of prepared La3+ion-BSA-AuNCs was calculated by using the following formula where quinine sulphate was used as a standard reference. The QY of La3+ion-BSA-AuNCs was found to be 18.20%.
Q
NCs
=
Q R
·
I
NCs
I R
·
A R
A
NCs
·
η
NCs
2
η R 2
where ‘Q’ stands for the QY, ‘I’ denotes the integrated fluorescent intensity. ‘A’ stands for the absorbance and ‘η’ for refractive index of the solvent used. The subscript ‘NCs’ represents nanoclusters and ‘R’ represents the reference compounds used for the estimation of QY.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of four divalent metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: Stock solutions of metal ions (1.0 mM) were prepared by dissolving corresponding metal nitrate salts in 0.1 M of HNO3 and further diluted with deionized water. For fluorescence analysis, the different metal ions (Hg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cr3+ and As3+, 0.5 mM) were added to La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs solutions separately and vortexed for 1.0 min. The fluorescence spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs measured by applying excitation wavelength at 323 nm. Upon the addition of Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+ ions, the emission peak at 652 nm was quenched whereas Cd2+ ion was enhanced the emission peak of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs. These spectral characteristics revealed that La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs could be used as a probe for assay of four divalent metal ions. The fluorescence detection of each meal ion was performed in triplicate for each concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of four divalent metal ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To illustrate the applicability of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescence method, tap water, canal water (Gujarat Agricultural Biotechnology Institute, Surat) and river water (Tapi river, Surat) were collected and filtered using micro filter (0.45 µm). All of the collected water samples were spiked with four divalent metal ions separately at different concentrations (0.1, 0.5 and 1.0 µM for Hg2+ ion, 0.1, 0.25 and 0.5 µM for Cu2+ ion, 2.5, 5.0 and 7.5 µM for Pb2+ ion, and 5.0, 7.5 and 10.0 µM for Cd2+ ion). The amount of four divalent metal ions was estimated by using calibration graphs and the obtained recoveries were tabulated in Supporting Information of Tables S1-S4.

The concentrations of Au3+ ion, NaOH and BSA were chosen based on the reported study in the literature [47–48], however, we have slightly modified the procedure by applying microwave irradiation and time. In this study, HAuCl4 (10.0 mM), BSA (125 mg/mL), NaOH (1.0 M), microwave irradiation (120 W) and time (100 s) were selected as optimum parameters for the fabrication of red fluorescent BSA-AuNCs. In order to investigate the role of La3+ ion in enhancing the fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs, the different concentrations of La3+ ion (0.5, 1.0, 2.0, 3.0, 4.0 and 5.0 mM) were introduced into BSA-AuNCs and the spectra (absorption and fluorescence) of AuNCs were depicted in Fig. S1 of Supporting Information. It was noticed that the fluorescence intensity of BSA-AuNCs was reached maximum by using 3.0 mM of La3+ ion (Fig. 2 and Fig. S1 of Supporting Information). Thus, 3.0 mM of La3+ ion was selected as an optimum concentration for the preparation of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs.

The fluorescent AuNCs were generated by the reduction of Au3+ ion to Au0 with controlled manner. The use of BSA as a ligand offers several features including effective encapsulation of Au3+ ion and more number of active functional groups to coordinate La3+ ion. It was observed that the addition of lanthanide ions (Y3+, Er3+ and Yb3+) into Au(I)NCs enhances the charge transfer fluorescence [49]. Based of this, the red fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs is generally increased due to the hybridization of AuNCs with La3+ ions, signifying the local structural variations (formation of La-O bonds) may induce the fluorescence enhancement of AuNCs [50]. Further, La3+ ion was incorporated into AuNCs via the bonding of La3+ ion with oxygen containing functional groups of AuNCs, favouring to assist effective electron or hole distribution, which lowers the energy gap and enhances charge transfer [51]. In view of this, the addition of La3+ ion into BSA-AuNCs enhances the fluorescence properties, which could allow them to use as a probe for the sensing of trace level analytes.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of four divalent metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To develop a fluorescence method for the analysis of multiple divalent metal ions, the fluorescence intensity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was tested in the presence of various metal ions (Hg2+, Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Pb2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cr3+ and As3+, 0.5 mM) by fluorescence spectroscopy. As depicted in Fig. 4 , La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs display a strong fluorescence at 652 nm when excited at λexc = 323. However, the emission spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs are quenched only by the addition of three divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) whereas Cd2+ ion is significantly enhanced the emission peak of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs. Remarkably, the fluorescent behaviour of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs did not undergo significant spectral changes in the presence of other metal ions (Ni2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Ag+, Cr3+ and As3+). These results demonstrated that La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs could be used as a nanosensor for fluorescence assay of four divalent metal ions via fluorescence quenching (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) and fluorescence enhancement (Cd2+ ion) mechanisms.

The fluorescence quenching of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was observed within 30 s upon the addition of three divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) (Fig. S7 of Supporting Information). Several investigations revealed that the detection of Hg2+, Pb2+ and Cu2+ ions is based on the fluorescence quenching via aggregation induced mechanism [3,24,38]. For Hg2+ ion; the emission intensity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was quenched due to the formation of Au-Hg nanoaggregations via d10-d10 interactions [38]. For Cu2+ ion, the quenching of emission peak of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs with Cu2+ ion was attributed to aggregation of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs induced by the formation of complexation between BSA-AuNCs and Cu2+ ion. As a result, Cu2+ ion could be stimulated the aggregation of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs through AuNCs linking process via the formation of Cu2+ ion-La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs complexes. For Pb2+ ion; the fluorescence quenching of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was mainly attributed to the metallophilic interaction between Au+ ion (5d10) and Pb2+ (5d10), which favors to form Pb2+ ion- La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs aggregations. For Cd2+ ion; the fluorescence peak at 652 nm was enhanced with the addition of Cd2+ ion. The fluorescence enhancement is due to the formation of Cd2+@La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs. Since Cd2+ ion may interact with Au core of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs, resulting to form complexes between Cd2+ ion and La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs, which yields to enhance fluorescence of AuNCs via aggregation induced emission.

In order to investigate the morphology and size of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs after the addition of four divalent metal ions, HR-TEM and DLS studies were performed and shown in Fig. 1d, e and S2b,c of Supporting Information. As displayed in Fig. 1a, La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs are well-dispersed with regular spherical shapes and their mean size is 1.37 ± 0.48 nm. After the introduction of divalent metal ions (Hg2+ and Pb2+), the mean size of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs showed obvious enlargements with average sizes of 5. 0 ± 1.2 and 7.45 ± 2.2 nm for Hg2+ and Pb2+ions, respectively (Fig. 1d and e). In addition, as displayed in Fig. S2, the mean hydrodynamic diameter of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was 4.65 ± 1.3 nm, however, the hydrodynamic diameter of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was increased to ̴ 6.2 ± 1.7 and 7.2 ± 1.9 nm after the addition of Hg2+ and Pb2+ions, respectively. These results confirmed that the morphology and size of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs were greatly affected by the addition of four divalent metal ions, which could be favoured to use them as a sensor for assaying of four divalent metal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assay of four divalent metal ions using La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs
ce:section.ce:para:
To establish analytical sensitivity, the degree of fluorescence quenching and enhancement of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was investigated by the independent addition of different concentrations of four divalent metal ions (Figs. 5 and 6
). The fluorescence intensities of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs were gradually decreased with increasing the concentrations of Hg2+ (0.01–500 µM), Cu2+ (0.075–500 µM) and Pb2+ (1.0–500 µM), respectively. Accordingly, the red fluorescence intensity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was progressively decreased with increasing concentration of three divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) (Insets of Figs. 5 and 6). Similarly, the fluorescence sensing of Cd2+ ion using La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was carried out as per the described procedure below. Upon the addition of different concentrations of Cd2+ ion (5–500 µM), a significant enhancement was observed in the emission intensity at 652 nm (excitation at 323 nm) with increasing concentration of Cd2+ ion (Fig. 6b) and the red fluorescence has become a more bright red fluorescence with increasing concertation under UV light (Inset of Fig. 6b). As a result, linear relationships were obtained by plotting calibration graphs between the ratio of fluorescence intensity I0/I (where “I0” displays the emission intensity at 652 nm without analyte and “I” represents the emission intensity at 652 nm with metal ions) at 652 nm and the concentrations in the range of 0.05–15, 0.05–10 and 2.5–60 µM for Hg2+ (R2 = 0.9914), Cu2+ (R2 = 0.99) and Pb2+ (R2 = 0.9899) ions, respectively (Figs. S8 and S9a of Supporting Information). Similarly, a linear relationship was noticed from the calibration plot of emission intensity (I/I0) as a function of the concentration of Cd2+ ion over the range of 5–50 µM (R2 = 0.9905) (Fig. S9b of Supporting Information). The detection limits are 0.02, 0.048, 0.19 and 4.93 µM for Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions, respectively. The obtained detection limits are lower than the maximum permissible levels of the above divalent metal ions (0.002 mg/L, 20 μM, 75 nm, and 3 μg/L for Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions) in drinking water recommended by U.S. EPA.

The analytical performance of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescence method was compared with the reported methods for the detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions) (Table 1 ). These results indicated that the present method exhibited lower detection limits than the reported methods [3,4,24,32,38,52–63] and comparable with the other analytical techniques [12–17]. Even though the present method exhibited lower sensitivity than the other analytical techniques (ICP- OES and MS) [12–17], but they are associated with substantial instrument cost, tedious sample preparations, maintenance, and required expensive solvent and ligands. Overall, the developed La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescence method exhibited several analytical features such as simplicity, miniaturization and sensitivity for parallel analysis of four divalent metal ions at microvolume of samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs and masking target metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the selectivity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs for Hg2+and Pb2+ ions, the fluorescence recognition of two divalent metal ions (Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions) using La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs as a nanosensor was investigated in the presence of various inorganic and organic species. Fig. S10 of Supporting Information showed that the fluorescence spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs in the presence of coexisting anions (NO3 −, SO4 2−, S2−, Cr2O7 2−, I−), metal ions (Ni2+, Mn2+, Cr3+, Co2+ and As3+) and pesticides (quinalphos, chlopropham, monocrotophos, and thiram) with and without four target divalent metal ions separately. These results demonstrated that the emission spectra of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs did not show any significant quenching or enhancement with the addition of other interfering metal ions, anions and organic molecules. However, two divalent metal ions (Hg2+ and Pb2+) showed drastic emission intensity quenching even in the presence of above interfering ions and molecules. These results revealed that the addition of two divalent metal ions separately into La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs may promote the significant spectral changes, confirming the selectivity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs towards four divalent metal ions in the coexistence of other chemical species.

In order to improve the selectivity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs towards one divalent metal ion in the presence of remaining three divalent metal ions, thiocyanate, 3-mercaptopropeionic acid, thiourea and KI were used as masking agents for Hg2+, Pb2+, Cu2+ and Cd2+ ions, respectively (Fig. 7 and Fig. S11 of Supporting Information). It can be noticed that the interference of Cu2+ and Cd2+ metal ions were masked by using thiourea and KI as masking agents during the Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions sensing, however Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions were not masked by using thiocyanate and 3-mercaptopropeionic acid masking agent (Fig. S11 of Supporting Information), demonstrating the selectivity of La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs as a fluorescent nanoprobe for the detection of Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions in the presence of other Cu2+ and Cd2+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence assays of four divalent metal ions in water samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate whether the La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescence method is applicable to the analysis of four divalent metal ions in water (tap water, canal water and river water) or not. Four divalent metal ions were spiked with different concentrations (Hg2+ (0.1, 0.5 and 1.0 µM), Cu2+ (0.1, 0.25 and 0.5), Pb2+ (2.5, 5.0 and 7.5 µM) and Cd2+ (5.0, 7.5 and 10.0 µM)) and analysed as per the described procedure earlier. The mean recoveries were in the range of 96.6–101.5%, 97.2–100.4%, 99.03–100.8% and of 99.6–100.4% for Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions, respectively with the relative standard deviations (RSD) < 2% (Tables S1–S4 of Supporting Information). These results demonstrated that La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs-based fluorescence method is a promising analytical tool for the simultaneous analysis of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) in water samples.

In summary, La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs was introduced as a novel fluorescent nanosensor for parallel detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) in water samples. The red fluorescence of BSA-AuNCs was enhanced with the introduction of La3+ ion and used as a fluorescent sensor for the detection of three divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+ and Pb2+) via fluorescence quenching and of Cd2+ ion via fluorescence enhancement. The developed probe exhibited good linear ranges with detection limits of 0.02, 0.048, 0.19, and 4.93 µM for Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ ions, respectively. The developed probe demonstrated good selectivity for the sensing of four divalent metal ions in the presence of other chemical species. Further, the interferences from other three target metal ions were selectively masked using masking agents, facilitating to detect single target divalent metal ion. Thus, La3+ ion-BSA-AuNCs could be used as a promising fluorescent nanoprobe for the simultaneous detection of four divalent metal ions (Hg2+, Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+) in water samples.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors grateful thank the Board of Research in Nuclear Sciences (BRNS) project (37(2)/14/07/2015/BRNS/10401), Department of Atomic Energy, Government of India, India. MLD and SKK thank the Director, SVNIT, Surat for providing necessary facilities.

Kailasa, Simple and sensitive colorimetric sensing of Cd2+ ion using chitosan dithio carbamate functionalized gold nanoparticles as a probe, Sens.
